div.p: There are no occurrences of wicäna n.
Check up again on words for 'leg', etc.
Check up on word for " shoulder "
or is there another word for " waist "?
ka´ta niniwo´hkukani = where I go fishing
ta´laninitawohku po´kani = I see the fishing-pole
yaka po´'ǫ = come, I want to see you
'aha´ po´wą ya´kani = No, I want to see you.
I'm picking the seeds out of cotton
" fore legs " of a horse, cow, cat, etc.
" rear legs " of a horse, cow, cat, etc.
hištaha lapu ya´kani = I'm doing pretty good
Can -'a´htca be used with static verbs ?
a broken bottle, dish, cup, etc.
ka´paš wo´tuhk´iti = we're feeding the chickens
wo´hkukani = I'm putting on my head
ta´hahkamu´tci ta´hkatomu ya´ša pu´htik'ura = the bread is rising
'iya´w'a´ki = she turned me down, she refused me
hu´ri yo´wihk'a´ta = the wind is blowing, waving; it's windy
yu´rika mi´li = medicinal root, purifies blood, boiled and mixed with whisky
Work to be done with Sesostrie
Have him retell most of the things under ethnological data
See list of verb stems & past participles
How do you say : I make it loose
Story of Natchez battle with the French (?)
mi´hkun'a´hkini = I put something around my head, body
wi´hkun'a´hkini = I let the blood run (this was done on the legs to make a good runner)
mi´hka = something you wear on your head (like a ribbon tied around the head)
ti´rasani e´sį ri´hč'ɔ´hkini = I tied a handkerchief around my head
ti´rasą e´sį ri´hč'ɔ´hkini = I tied a handkerchief around my head
ti´rasą miht'ɔ´hkini = I tied it around my head
ta´wohku wo´hk'ɔhkini = I wear a hat
tɛ´hkal 'a´rani = I'm laying down in the middle
tehiya´wą ya´kani = I want to make around
ta´hɔhka ki´ču wä´s'ahkini = I jump into a hole
hɔ´hka wä´r'ahkini = I dig a hole
ta´hɔhka le´hp'ä´hkini = I shut up the hole
čir'aki = it scatters, splashes (like hot grease)
ta´maka čir'aki = the grease flys off
ta´maka ru´wina ya´hk'a´rani = I warm the grease
i´stahpu mi´lihk'arani = I paint my face red
šulihk-hi´pu = Pumpkin Dance (used to have that kind)
e´riništuk'ɔhɔ = it's too heavy, I can't lift it
ta´rowinak sä´yakani = I tear the paper
ti´ra sä´yakani = I'm tearing some cloth
ti´ra po´htukani = I'm cutting some cloth
ka´nahh 'erisah'ähkini = I tote something on my back
ri´'į hu´marɔ´wahk'arani = I paint the houses white
sihkamo´ya = mole, lives in pine woods
ti´ra me´lihk´a'rani = I blacken the cloth
ti´ra mi´lihk´a'rani = I make the cloth red
ti´ra ka´i me´lihk´a'rani = I dye the cloth yellow (?)
yuriši mi´li = root for making red powder
ha´hka ki´rikani = I'm grinding some corn
ha´hka to´mukani = I'm beating some corn
wiši ku´makani = I take a sip of water
ru´wišiya = black bird with red on wing
he´rį ma´šukani = I make a pirogue
ka´nahk 'uhäšamo´lukani = I pour something in & fill it up
ri´hku hä´san'a´hkinni = I sawed the wood
ta´ninik 'uwohkųn ya´paku = when I fish the fish bites
ya´r'uhki = it has dried up (like bayou, ocean)
ta´tira huw'a´hkį si´h'ɔki = the cloth I washed got dry
ka´nahk 'ate´pį yuru ya'a´hkini = I make it long
ka´nahk 'ate´pį ti´ka ya'a´hkini = I make it large
'ä´ska li´sukani = I slide on my feet
ya´i pa´t'uhki = it slips out of (my hands) and falls
ka´nahk he´lų ya´'ara = edge of anything
hɔ´hkahe´lų wär'ahkini = I dig a hole
ta´ik wä´haku = the fire makes a light
ta´hč'i li´kakati = the sun is shining
ka´nahk yu´r'ɔhɔ yakani = I make it short
ka´nahk ko´ču ya´hk'a´rani = I make it short
ka´paš 'o´ruhk 'učo´runik´ahča = I'm going to twist (wring) the chicken's neck
wa´čukani = I'll pull it up (e.g. grass)
ka´nahk ha´iht 'upita päcLtan'a´hkini = I mash something, walking on it
ka´nahk ha´iht 'upita ku´čan'a´hkini = I mash something, walking on it
ta´narak ta´Rku ta´piku´ran'uhki = the snake coils around a tree
ta´yųka m'aku´ran 'ato´lun'ahkini = I wind up the cord
maitoru ma´sipo´kani = I turn around and look
wilakani = I weave (a basket, wicker-chair, etc.)
ta´šikunik po´htuku = the knife cuts, is sharp
ma´likani = I am in the process of learning
ti´rasą mi´htakani = I wear a handkerchief on my head
so´htun'ahkini = I put it in a stooped position
ka´na 'uworuhtanik'ahča = I teach him something; I show him something
'umi´ran'a´hkini = I show him before a mirror
tasähapok 'uhču´nimą 'umi´ran'ahkini = I take a mirror and show him
haso´htų 'u´k'ähkini = I sit having it stooped down; humped over?
'uta´man'a´hkini = I live (go) together with him; I associate with him
'uta´ran'a´hkini = I put it out (up) straight (like hanging curtains), spread out (on floor)
ti´hkakati = it bursts (like a boil, balloon, etc.)
tu´huku = to hook, e.g. cow (with horns)
pa´hkakani = I dress in a decorated manner
ta´yak 'uta´hką lu´p'uhki = the deer lies stretched out dead
ta´čiyak 'utɔhkahtą 'ura´p'ɔhkini = I shot and killed a squirrel
ta´čiyak 'utɔhką 'ura´p'ɔhkini = I shot and killed a squirrel
kɔ´ra = flat and round (like a dollar)
ka´nahk 'uka´han'ahkini = I pay my debt to him
ka´nahk 'uni´'ä´hkini = I say something to him
katotų šuwikani = I scatter it all about
sa´haku = he raises his arm to throw something
hasa´hiku = he throws it over his shoulder
hasa´h'uhki = he throws it over his shoulder
so´liku = he sneaks up on it; he approaches it
ku´wa so´l'uhki = he sneaks up on a duck
ka´nahk la´p'i´wana = I want to sell something
ka´na ha´naya´wą ya´kani = I borrow something
ka´nahk 'uha´n'a´hkini = I lend something to him
ta´yanihč tiri´hču to´w'uhki = he's tying the cow
la´mikani = I draw (hide), (e.g. hide in tanning process)
ti´ra li´su la´mikani = I'm ironing cloth
ri´hkuto´lu le´puku = he's bending the stick
ka´na ra´p'uhki = in ans. to killer
ta´paru wi´wuhk'a´ra = the cloud is moving
to´nik wi´wuhk'ura = the person is waving
'upi´htuni tɔ´rahk'u´ta = he's drunk and staggering around
ta´tira wi´wuhk'a´raj ha´iš = the flag is waving
ta´'oniR pä´k'a´hkini = I hit the horns
ta´lah tipäk'a´hkini = I hit the arrow
'akawa´ha = squash bug (must be that says S.)
to´stohok 'umä´kakani = I fuss with the child
to´stohok wi´rataha 'umä´kakano = I fuss with bad child
tɔ´hkali päk´ahkini = I hit the sack
'a´paru sačiwik´a'hča = it's cloudy and is going to rain
lɔ´'patä´rą = some kind of root (something like cabbage) extinct now; leaves stay green all winter
ha´'atani = they come to borrow something
ha´hpaya ya´kani = I make a noise
ha´li pu´sakani = I break up the ground
ha´hkato´mu su´hpį rau´'ɔhkini = I put the bread to rise
ha´li ma´kikani = I plow the ground
ta´rihč mi´ši ka´l'ära = the house is over there
hi´nahk´ɔhča = it's going to be that way
ro´wina wo´runi ču´'ɔ´hkini = I have book-knowledge
'o´ni hi´sawa = a person is a slave
yu´sawa = bark used for making black dye, plane tree (la plän); green chestnut hulls mixed with plane-tree bark
(can't remember name of root for making red dye)
(If you kill one, you're rich, 'cause he has diamonds)
hɔ´są-me´li = la liane noise (has pad de chat (cat grapes))
hɔ´są-ta´wuli = poison sumac (grows in the swamp); (something points)
wa´toruhki = extinct now (coulee de gris, Bayou named from these birds; don't come any more)

tr.td: marrying her he took her and went
marrying her he took her and went

div.p: 'ä´ča = crow black woodpecker (Creole pique bois noire)
ka´nahk hɔ´hka 'upa´nun'a´hkini = to slip through (string through crack)
ta´wi´ši pɔ´htati mu´htikati = when you boil water it steams
ri´hkuwi´la = sound willow tree/sound waving tree
čä´ra = notched (like straw hat on brim)
'upolehe = he looks like someone else/he favors someone
ta´roptį 'a´sa rau´kani = I separate and place the cotton
'upu´htikani = clean, dust, separate, fluff cotton (for making quilt)
ta´roptį puhtin'ɔ̨ta = they remove seeds from cotton
'i´mą 'e´lu = I'm used to it, like the place
ta´tɔrahk sačik'ara = the hail is raining, it's hailing
te´lu ya´kati = she made a noise
se´m'ä´kini = I came back to return
či´mikani = I stir up the fire, I poke the fire
čohkuku = he's chopping, he's pecking (of bird)
mą lɔ´t'ik'i = run! let you run!
su´suwi = hawk species (eat snakes, pointed wings, white and dark-colored, gray)
ka´šku lɔ´takata = how many were running?
ki´rukani = I'm rasping/scraping with a file
ku´wale´he = dove or pigeon species (bigger than pigeon, gray yellow in color)
ta´nini la´katasiti = the fish lay eggs
ku´ma = in story about 'a´hpasala´mihta VI 33 p. 176 of text
ti´hči tatipo´lehä´ni tašlepa´nɔni = she looks better, she is very beautiful
Addendum to How and What the Indians Used to Hunt

tr.td: when they split and split it (fire) again
when they split and split it (fire) again

div.p: ši´mitahk = they were playing all the time
lɔ´takutahk = he was running all the time
ha´rata´hk'a´'aki = she was singing all the time
ti´hčita´hki = she was single all the time
The Origin of Baou de Glaisse (Second Telling)

tr.td: they were fussing all the time
they were fussing all the time

div.p: ta´tišuma sa´mikani = I'm roasting the meat
ru´wina ya´hi´lauk = it's a little warm
ya´ 'ili ru´štą 'e´nihku = I see two deer and three rabbits
ka´'aš yu´kat'i yak'i k´ahča = How long you stay before you come?
'a´ha 'a´yähäšp'aha ya´kanik'a´hča = I'll be back before long.
ta´nakata´watani = warrior-counselor, worm that lives in rotten tree
ta´šilata´watani = warrior-counselor, worm that lives in rotten tree
'ǫ´rɔwaska´šį ya´napo´kį = you learn how to speak French?
2nd house in 3rd block on left back of high school on the road to Aleck
yo´rǫ ya´na'i´wana = I want to speak Tunica
yo´rǫ Tunica n ya´nawą ya´kani v.I
no´kuš 'e´nihku po´kani = I see three beans
ru´štą no´kušį 'o´hkų po´kani = I see a rabbit, a bear, and some children
ru´štą no´kušį ko´hkų po´kani = I see a rabbit, a bear, and a turtle
ta´yanišsi´nimą sįpo´kani = I'm looking at the cows
ta´Rku the woods n ka´l'ura 'učohku wa´kan´ahkini
kɔ´ra sa´hku 'i´wana = I want one drink
ta´'ɔhtali ye´htuk'arani = I untie the sack

tr.td: (I guess he was inviting them to eat)
(I guess he was inviting them to eat)

div.p: (Avoyelles came out from a place near Mansura)
The commander was under the chief and commanded the people.
If they had a feast, he would speak to the people.
hi´nahku adv ka´ta pro-form hɔ´hka n
tɔ´hkaLĕ 'uto´wile´hp'ähkini = I tie the sack shut
šu´hpa le´hp'uhki = it shuts by pressing
h'iyu´ri po´htu la´m'aki = my cut healed
h'iyu´ri po´htu la´male´hp'äki = my cut healed
ti´šuki le´hp'ähkini = I shut the door
ti´šuki šu´hp'aki = the door is shut
ka´nahk 'u´k'ɔ̨ta hi´štahahk hi´nahk po´katä´ni = Where they stayed it still looks like that
Find out what difference, if any,
ʔo´nrɔwaka´šin ya´napo´kǐn = you learn to speak French?
Addendum to How and what the Indians used to Hunt... 123
The Origin of Bayou des Glaise (2nd telling)... 139-153
The Migration of the Tunica (2nd telling of
the Tunica and Spanish Defeat the Avoyelles)... 163-187
